text:
Traffic flow prediction is one of the core components of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). Due to the complex spatial-temporal relationships between various traffic-related factors in urban areas, the task remains difficult. Currently, the Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely adopted for traffic forecasting. They, however, possess the following drawbacks: 1) The static spatial structures are not robust to dynamically changing traffic patterns in different time periods and are not adaptive to varying road characteristics, which reduces the models’ predictive performance for the latter periods and for roads with non-homogeneous attributes. 2) The limited-range relationships within the graph structure ignore the potential long-range semantic links, which have been reported to be beneficial in promoting the global representations and are widely adopted in vision-related models. 3) The instantaneous information diffusion between pairs of nodes is not aligned with the real physical distances between different locations and does not fully capture the inherent spatial distances and their impact on traffic developments.

Recent literature has shown that employing deep learning approaches to arterial traffic speed forecasting can significantly outperform traditional methods. Nevertheless, current solutions face a number of drawbacks including being either computationally expensive, or highly complex, thus limiting their practical applications. In order to tackle these shortcomings, this paper proposes a Propagation Delay-aware dynamic long-range Transformer named PDFormer. It enhances the forecasting performance by utilizing a spatial self-attention mechanism to dynamically discover the varying dependencies at different scales through a combination of geographic and semantic masking matrices. A traffic delay-aware feature transformation module is adopted to model the propagation delay caused by the time period for the changes in the actual traffic speed to be transferred from one sensor to another. Experimental results on six large-scale public arterial traffic speed datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed PDFormer model in terms of both forecasting accuracy and computational cost. Visualization of the model’s attention maps further confirms the interpretability of the underlying urban traffic interactions captured by the proposed network.
